topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom felt suddenly thin, suffocating. I had been nursing a lukewarm champagne flute, trying to appear serenely engaged in a conversation about the caterer, when I saw him. Standing near the punch bowl, laughing with a group of people I vaguely recognized, was Mark. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I watched him approach, and the warmth in my chest instantly curdled into something cold and sharp. When he turned and his eyes locked onto mine, there was a flicker of something—a fleeting acknowledgment—that made my breath hitch. I forced a tight, brittle smile, my hand gripping the glass so hard my knuckles were white. I made a deliberate move toward the exit, needing distance, needing to escape the suffocating proximity of his presence.

[story 2]

"Oh, wow, look who it is!" Sarah chirped, nudging me forward. I managed a strained laugh, my gaze fixed firmly on the intricate floral arrangement on the wall. Then I saw her. Across the room, bathed in the soft glow of the string lights, was Chloe. My entire posture shifted; I felt a sudden, intense need to physically recoil. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples. I wanted to pivot, to disappear into the crowd, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot. When Chloe caught my eye, I immediately looked down, fiddling obsessively with the edge of my napkin, my breathing growing shallow and rapid. I could feel a distinct heat rising in my cheeks, a furious, internal pressure building behind my eyes. I needed to find an excuse, a sudden, urgent reason to vacate this space.

[story 3]

I was mid-story, recounting a ridiculous anecdote about a past vacation, when the ambient noise of the reception seemed to drop away. It was him. Standing near the dance floor, looking effortlessly polished, was Alex. A wave of sheer, unadulterated displeasure washed over me, making my skin feel prickly. My voice, when I finally spoke, was unnaturally level, clipped. "Excuse me," I managed, the words sounding brittle even to my own ears. I didn't wait for a response. I simply turned on my heel, the movement sharp and abrupt, pushing past a cluster of guests. The feeling that seized me wasn't just annoyance; it was a profound sense of being invaded, a raw, burning indignation that demanded immediate severance from the scene. I walked toward the periphery, determined to create a physical barrier between myself and everything he represented.
